
### looks like canaries? (1)  {.smaller}


* what attacks does this stop that canaries don't? .5cm
* ID does not need to be secret! 

   * assuming non-executable writeable memory, no!
   * attacker can't write new places for return to go

* avoids ‘‘stack pivoting’’ attacks 

   * attacker can't make stack pointer point to wrong part of stack\ldots
   * and expect it to return differently



### looks like canaries? (2)  {.smaller}



* what attacks does this NOT stop that canaries do?
* example: SortList can be called from Innocent, <br> then return from Dangerous  

   * assumption: attacker can overwrite return address at right time (running on another core? problem with sortFunc1?)

 
:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
 
```
void Innocent() {
  ...
  SortList(someList1,
           sortFunc1);
  Use(someList1);
  ...
}

```
 
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
 
```
void Dangerous() {
  ...
  SortList(someList2,
           sortFunc2);
  UseDangerously(someList2);
  ...
}

```
 
:::

::::
